name: Madhva
dates_notes: Inscription, maṭha records, near-contemporary biographies (Maṇi-Mañjarī, Madhva-Vijaya); among the best-attested medieval Indian philosophers.

dates_evidence.description: Contemporary or near-contemporary inscriptions in the Tuluva region attest his lifetime.
Manuscript colophons across the Sarva-Mūla corpus.

lineage_polemical.note: The Tattvavāda position is the principal target of Madhusūdana's Advaita-Siddhi (via Vyāsatīrtha).
Madhva's Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana / Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana / Upādhi-Khaṇḍana triad explicitly targets the Advaitic position attributed to Śaṅkara.

Madhva's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya and Tattva-Viveka treat the Cārvāka position as the limit-case of nāstika error: the denial of post-mortem soul, of inference, and of śabda-pramāṇa together constitute the most thorough rejection of the Vedāntic frame.

Madhva is unusual among Vedāntins in redeploying Vaiśeṣika categories positively — viśeṣas are central to his ontology of internal differentiation within otherwise-identical entities (svagata-bheda); the relation is constructive on padārtha-theory, polemical on atomistic cosmology.

core_thesis:
Madhva organises Vedānta around one grammatical asymmetry. At *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.2, he reads the sūtra *janmādyasya yataḥ* as fixing Viṣṇu's status as *svatantra* (independent-of-being, owing his being to nothing else) and everything else as *paratantra*, owing its being, knowing, and acting to him. The two terms are taken at face value. *Svatantra-bhāva* is not a Cartesian-substance claim about non-interaction; it is a claim about ontological asymmetry of dependence, expressed in the *bhāṣya* through the grammar of the genitive and the ablative. Whatever depends does not generate; whatever generates does not depend.

The second pillar is *pañca-bheda*: five differences (between *Brahman* and *jīva*, *Brahman* and *jaḍa* (insentient matter), *jīva* and *jīva*, *jīva* and *jaḍa*, and *jaḍa* and *jaḍa*) that are real, permanent, and undissolved in *mokṣa*. Madhva's argument is that no construal of any one of the five as merely conventional (*vyāvahārika*) is consistent with the asymmetric-dependence sūtras of *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3 read in their natural order. The five are not premises; they are what the *Sūtra*-text, taken word by word, requires.

The distinctive technical device is *viśeṣa*: a self-distinguishing relational element that allows real intra-substantial differentiation without positing further entities to do the differentiating. Madhva introduces *viśeṣa* in the *Anuvyākhyāna* and the *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna* to answer a precise objection: that to distinguish *guṇa* from *guṇin* one must either multiply substances or collapse them. *Viśeṣa* refuses both horns. It is the school's most original logical instrument and is what blocks the regress against which *bheda* would otherwise have to defend itself.

*Jīvas* form a hierarchy of three classes: *mukti-yogya* (fit for release), *nitya-saṃsārī* (eternally transmigrating), *tamo-yogya* (fit for darkness). The classification is metaphysical, not pedagogical. Liberation is *aparokṣa-jñāna* (direct knowledge) of Viṣṇu, mediated by *bhakti* and never collapsing into identity. Madhva's frequent claim that "you are not Viṣṇu" (e.g. *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna*, *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana*) is the denial of an identity-thesis under its specific Advaitin formulations, not a generic anti-monist slogan; what the denial accomplishes within Madhva's own dialectic should not be assimilated to what some later Advaitin denial of *ahaṅkāra*-identity accomplishes within its.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sutra-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary:
Madhva's prose commentary on the *Brahma-Sūtra*. It is the shortest of the major Vedānta *sūtra-bhāṣyas* and proceeds *adhikaraṇa* by *adhikaraṇa*, fixing each topic-unit by a sequence of *viṣaya*, *saṃśaya*, *pūrvapakṣa*, *siddhānta*, and *saṅgati*.

The controlling reading is set at *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.2. Madhva takes *janmādyasya yataḥ* ("that from which the origin and the rest of this [world]") as fixing Viṣṇu as *svatantra*, independent-of-being, and the world as *paratantra*, dependent on him. *Brahman* is read as exclusively Viṣṇu; *jagat-kāraṇa* is given a realist sense, with *upādāna* not as material constituent but as the locus on which the world depends. Śaṅkara's identity-reading of *upādāna* is rejected on the grammatical ground that the *Sūtra*-language does not bear it.

Three philological choices then govern the rest. First, *ānandamaya* (Taittirīya 2.5) is read as Viṣṇu, not as the *jīva*-self; the *adhikaraṇa* is settled at *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.12–19. Second, the difference-sūtras of 2.3 are read in *taratamya*-key: where Śaṅkara had read 2.3.43 (the *aṃśa-adhikaraṇa*) as identity-with-modification, Madhva reads it as asymmetric-dependence. Third, *neti neti* (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 2.3.6) is read as eliminating defects in *Brahman*, not as eliminating predication of *Brahman*. The philological choice is small. Its systematic consequences are large.

The *bhāṣya* is the seed-text on which the *Anuvyākhyāna*, Jayatīrtha's *Tattva-Prakāśikā* and *Nyāya-Sudhā*, Vyāsatīrtha's *Tātparya-Candrikā*, and Rāghavendra's *Pariṃala* successively build.

engaged_works.title: Anuvyakhyana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary:
A verse *vārttika* on Madhva's own *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*: roughly two thousand *anuṣṭubh* stanzas across four *adhyāyas*, written to expand and to argue what the *bhāṣya* asserts in compressed form. The *bhāṣya* states the position; the *Anuvyākhyāna* mounts the dialectical apparatus.

For each contested *adhikaraṇa*, Madhva sets out a *pūrvapakṣa* (drawn from Advaitins, the Bhāskarīyas, Jainas, Buddhists, and Cārvākas) and answers it through formal *anumāna*-statements coordinated with *śruti* and with *anubhava* (direct experience). The treatment of *bheda* at *Anuvyākhyāna* 1.4.111–112 is representative: Madhva argues that *bheda* is *svarūpa-svabhāva* (constitutive of the natures of the relata), so that the standard regress-objection ("a further *bheda* would be needed to differentiate *bheda* from its terms, *ad infinitum*") does not arise; *bheda* is not an extra item in need of further differentiation.

Three topics are developed here more fully than anywhere else in Madhva's corpus. The first is *pratibimba*: the *jīva* as image to Viṣṇu's original (*Anuvyākhyāna* 2.3.50–53, the *aṃśa*-*adhikaraṇa* verses), an asymmetric relation in which the image owes everything to the original and the original nothing to the image. The second is *viśeṣa* as the device that grounds intra-substantial differentiation (*Anuvyākhyāna* 2.3, *viśeṣa*-introducing verses). The third is *sākṣi*, witness-consciousness, treated as a self-luminous *pramāṇa* in its own right (*Anuvyākhyāna* 3.2 ff., the *pramāṇa*-*adhyāya*) and not reducible to inference or testimony.

From Jayatīrtha onward, the school takes the *Anuvyākhyāna*, rather than the *bhāṣya*, as its primary base-text for technical exegesis.

engaged_works.title: Gita-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary:
Madhva's prose commentary on the *Bhagavad-Gītā*, paired with the separate *Gītā-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*. Where Śaṅkara had read the *Gītā* as a propaedeutic to *jñāna* whose identity-statements are taken in identity-mode, Madhva reads it as Kṛṣṇa speaking in Viṣṇu's voice about *svatantra-paratantra* dependence.

The interpretive choice is sharpest at the apparent identity-locutions. *Aham ātmā guḍākeśa sarva-bhūtāśaya-sthitaḥ* (*Gītā* 10.20) is glossed not as "I am the self of all beings" in an identity sense but as "I am the self of all beings *as their inner controller and locus of dependence*" — *bimba-pratibimba*, original and image. The grammar of the locative *sarva-bhūtāśaya-sthitaḥ* is taken to license dependence rather than identity. Similarly for *vāsudevaḥ sarvam iti* at *Gītā* 7.19: Vāsudeva is *all* in the sense that all depends on him, not in the sense that all is numerically identical with him.

*Karma*, *jñāna*, and *bhakti* are read as ordered means with one terminus: *aparokṣa-jñāna* of Viṣṇu, mediated by *bhakti*. The relevant verses of *Gītā* 9 (*ananyāś cintayanto mām*) and *Gītā* 18 (*sarva-dharmān parityajya*) carry the burden. *Jñāna* alone does not suffice; *bhakti* is the engine.

The *bhāṣya* draws repeatedly on *Pañcarātra* and *Mahābhārata*-affiliated citations whose conventional acceptance was contested (the so-called *Madhva-quotations*). Appayya Dīkṣita's later *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Bhañjana* turned this into a polemical front, and Vijayīndra answered it. The doctrinal effect of the *bhāṣya* is to fix the school's reading of *Gītā* 7, 9, 10, 13, and 15, chapters Śaṅkara had read in identity-mode, as dependence-mode.

engaged_works.title: Tattvodyota
engaged_works.summary:
A short polemical *prakaraṇa*, one of the *Daśa-Prakaraṇa* of the *Sarva-Mūla* corpus. Its target is identity (*aikya*) between *jīva* and *Brahman* in any of three forms: strict Advaitic *aikya*, Bhāskara-style *bhedābheda*, and *upādhi*-mediated identity.

The argument is a trilemma. Any *aikya* compatible with the difference *jīvas* manifestly experience would have to be (i) accidental, in which case identity-*śruti* is trivialised; or (ii) *upādhi*-based, in which case the position reduces to Bhāskara's, already refuted; or (iii) *vivarta*-based, in which case it presupposes a coherent *mithyātva*-doctrine that has not been supplied. The first horn cannot bear the soteriological weight Advaitins put on identity-statements. The second collapses into a different opponent. The third presupposes a thesis Madhva attacks separately in the *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana*.

The text gives some of Madhva's cleanest treatment of *sākṣi* as the *jīva*-particular self-luminous faculty by which difference is experienced and so cannot be reasoned away by mediated cognition (*Tattvodyota*, *bheda-anubhava* section, on the irreducibility of the *sākṣi*-given to inferential overruling). *Sākṣi* here is not a Cartesian inner-eye; it is a *pramāṇa* whose verdict precedes inference and whose verdicts inference cannot displace.

Jayatīrtha's substantial *Tattvodyota-Ṭīkā* is the standard sub-commentary.

engaged_works.title: Mayavada-Khandana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored polemic against the Advaitic māyā-doctrine.
engaged_works.summary:
A compact polemical *prakaraṇa* targeting the Advaitic claim that the world is *māyika* — neither *sat* nor *asat*, but *anirvacanīya*, indeterminable.

Madhva exhausts the disjunction. If *māyā* is *sat*, Advaita has two reals and self-refutes. If it is *asat*, it cannot produce the appearances Advaita assigns to it. If it is *anirvacanīya*, then either the predicate "*anirvacanīya*" is itself determinate, in which case *māyā* is determinate after all, or the predicate is *anirvacanīya*, in which case the doctrine cannot be stated without applying a determinate predicate to its own indeterminacy. The third horn is the live one for Advaita, and Madhva's pressure on it is the text's central argument.

Madhva is careful here on a point his opponents repeatedly try to exploit. *Mithyā* is not equivalent to *asat*: he denies the equation. But he argues that the Advaitic *mithyātva*, once distinguished from *asat*, still requires a coherent positive characterisation, and he denies that one has been supplied. The work is the immediate ancestor of the *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana* (which targets the *anumāna* by which Advaitins try to *prove* *mithyātva*) and of the *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana*. The three together constitute the anti-Advaita triad to which Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* is the eventual reply.

engaged_works.title: Mithyatvanumana-Khandana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored polemic against the Advaitic inference for mithyātva.
engaged_works.summary:
A focused attack on the formal Advaitic inference for the falsity of the world. The standard form is *vimataṃ mithyā, dṛśyatvāt, śuktirūpyavat*: "the disputed [world] is *mithyā*, because perceptible, like the silver-on-shell." Madhva dissects the inference *hetu* by *hetu*.

The argument has three principal moves. First, the *hetu* *dṛśyatva* (perceptibility) admits a counter-instance internal to Advaita: *Brahman*'s self-perception, which on Advaitin grounds cannot be *mithyā*. The inference is *vyabhicārī* (deviating). Second, the *sapakṣa* (the silver-on-shell) is itself controvertible: the sublation of the silver-perception is the rejection of a false predication of "silver" upon "this", not the ontological annihilation of a positive *sat-asat-vilakṣaṇa* entity. The Advaitin owes an account of what was sublated, and the *śukti-rajata* example does not supply one. Third, the inferred *mithyātva* is itself either *satya*, in which case it counter-exemplifies its own conclusion, or *mithyā*, in which case the inference is self-undermining (the *bādhita-viṣayatva* problem).

Madhva runs the same dialectic against each substitute *hetu*: *jaḍatva* (insentience), *paricchinnatva* (limitedness), and *bhāvarūpatva* (positive-existence). Each is taken in turn.

This is the technical scaffolding Vyāsatīrtha later refines into *Navya-Nyāya* form in the *Nyāyāmṛta*, and the chain of arguments Madhusūdana will labour to overturn in the *Advaita-Siddhi*.

engaged_works.title: Upadhi-Khandana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored polemic against the upādhi-mediation strategy.
engaged_works.summary:
The third panel of Madhva's anti-Advaita triad. The target is the *upādhi*-mediation strategy: the claim that *jīva* is *Brahman*-as-conditioned-by-some-*upādhi* (a conditioning factor — body, mind, *avidyā*), with non-difference recovered in *mokṣa* once the conditioning is removed. The strategy is fully explicit in the Bhāskara and Yādava-Prakāśa lines and tacit in the Advaitins' *avaccheda-vāda*.

Madhva runs a dilemma. Either the *upādhi* is real, or it is *mithyā*. If the *upādhi* is real, then two things genuinely differ by their conditioning factors, and the non-difference Advaitins want to reassert in *mokṣa* is merely verbal — there was no prior identity to which the relata could return. If the *upādhi* is *mithyā*, then the whole account presupposes a successful *mithyātva*-doctrine, which the *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana* has already denied. Either way the strategy fails.

A secondary argument addresses *upādhi*-talk as a category. *Upādhi* in classical Nyāya inference is a defeater that distinguishes a sound from an unsound *vyāpti*; it is not a metaphysical operator that makes one thing into another. To use *upādhi* metaphysically, as if a real condition could mediate identity-with-difference, is to import a logical term into ontology where it has no warrant.

The text closes the regress-route by which Advaitins might otherwise escape the previous two *Khaṇḍanas*.

engaged_works.title: Vishnu-Tattva-Vinirnaya
engaged_works.summary:
Madhva's most extended argument that the *param-Brahman* of *śruti* is Viṣṇu/Nārāyaṇa and no other deity. The argument has two phases.

The first phase concerns the scope of *śabda-pramāṇa*. Mīmāṃsakas and Naiyāyikas had restricted authoritative scriptural language in ways that excluded the *Pañcarātra* corpus and the *Ṛg-veda-khila* passages on which much of Madhva's positive theology relies. Madhva defends a broader scope, on the ground that *āpta-vākya* (authoritative utterance) is fixed by the speaker's epistemic standing rather than by conventional textual delimitation. The argument here is in some respects more conservative than Mīmāṃsā: he refuses to add any *human* author to the Vedic transmission, and he refuses to subtract from what the transmission already contains.

The second phase deploys *śabda*, *anumāna*, and *anubhava* together. Every cosmologically maximal predicate (*sarva-jña*, all-knowing; *sarva-śaktimat*, all-powerful; *sarva-niyantṛ*, universal controller; *svatantra*, independent) is shown to attach uniquely to Viṣṇu. Each is taken in turn. No rival construal of *Brahman* (impersonal, attribute-less, or co-instantiated by other devas) survives the joint pressure of the three *pramāṇa*s.

The work also gives Madhva's most explicit *taratamya* of devatās: Brahmā and Vāyu next under Viṣṇu, then Sarasvatī and Bhāratī, then Indra, and so on (*Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya*, the *devatā-tāratamya* section). Jayatīrtha's *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya-Ṭīkā* is the standard sub-commentary; its citation-fabric is the perennial locus of inter-school disputes over the identity of the *Vedānta*-Brahman.

engaged_works.title: Gita-Tatparya-Nirnaya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed as the doctrinal-summary companion to the Gītā-Bhāṣya in Madhva's mature period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored as part of the Sarva-Mūla-Granthāḥ; manuscript transmission and maṭha attestation uniform.
engaged_works.summary:
Companion to the *Gītā-Bhāṣya*. Where the *bhāṣya* glosses verses, the *Tātparya-Nirṇaya* establishes what the *Gītā* as a whole purports. The genre is *tātparya-nirṇaya*: a determination of doctrinal point, made by selecting key verses across all eighteen chapters and showing that the alternative monist and *karma*-mīmāṃsaka constructions cannot account for the selection together.

The settled purport, on Madhva's reading, is fourfold. Kṛṣṇa is Viṣṇu/Nārāyaṇa, the *param-Brahman* of *śruti*. The *jīva* is *paratantra*, ontologically dependent by *bimba-pratibimba* (original-and-image). *Karma*, *jñāna*, and *bhakti* are ordered means whose terminus is *aparokṣa-jñāna* of Viṣṇu, mediated by *bhakti*. The *vibhūti*-chapter (10), the *viśva-rūpa* (11), and the *kṣetra-kṣetrajña-vibhāga* (13) are all read in *taratamya*-key — that is, as articulating a graded dependence-hierarchy among real beings, not as collapsing them into identity.

The *Tātparya* is the standard internal reference for fixing the school's reading of any disputed *Gītā* verse, and the text most cited when Mādhvas argue against Advaitic and Viśiṣṭādvaita readings. It belongs in genre and method with the *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* and the *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*.

engaged_works.title: Mahabharata-Tatparya-Nirnaya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Late-mature work; one of Madhva's longest and most ambitious compositions.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; among the longest works of the Sarva-Mūla corpus.
engaged_works.summary:
A thirty-two-chapter verse work (roughly five thousand verses) determining the doctrinal purport of the *Mahābhārata*. It is Madhva's longest treatment of any text outside the *Brahma-Sūtra* corpus.

The doctrinal stake is large. By giving the *Mahābhārata* a *tātparya-nirṇaya* of the same kind he gives to *Gītā* and *Bhāgavata*, Madhva treats it as a *prasthāna*-text (a foundational textual base for the school's exegesis) rather than as illustrative narrative. The elevation is unusual within Vedānta. Its consequence is that the school's later disputes over contested epic episodes (the *Bhīṣma-Stuti*, the Kṛṣṇa-*viśva-rūpa*, the *Anugītā*) are settled by *Mahābhārata*-internal reading rather than by reference outside the text.

The theological reading is *taratamya*-graded throughout. The *Pāṇḍavas* are arranged as *jīvas* of differing rank under Kṛṣṇa's direct *anugraha* (favour), with Bhīma identified as the *avatāra* of Vāyu (*Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* 2, the *vāyu-avatāra* identification). This last identification is part of Madhva's *vāyu-tritaya* doctrine: Hanumān in the *Tretā*, Bhīma in the *Dvāpara*, Madhva himself in the *Kali* as the three *avatāras* of Mukhya-Prāṇa (*Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* 32, the closing *adhyāya*).

The text incorporates many citations from *Bhāratīya*-affiliated and *Pañcarātra* sources whose conventional acceptance has been disputed. The dispute over their authenticity is a recurring item in inter-school polemic; Vijayīndra later defends them in the *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Mardana*.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Sankhyana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; one of the *Daśa-Prakaraṇa* of the Sarva-Mūla corpus.
engaged_works.summary:
A short *prakaraṇa*, under thirty verses in the standard recension, enumerating the categories (*tattvas*) of *Tattvavāda* in graded order. The text is the school's positive ontological summary; the polemical *Daśa-Prakaraṇa* texts presuppose it.

The foundational division is binary. *Svatantra-tattva* (independent-of-being reality) is one: Viṣṇu. *Paratantra-tattva* (dependent reality) is everything else. The *paratantra* category is then subdivided into *cetana* (sentient) and *acetana* (insentient). The *cetanas* are subdivided into three *jīva*-classes (*mukti-yogya*, fit for release; *nitya-saṃsārī*, eternally transmigrating; *tamo-yogya*, fit for darkness), with internal sub-ordering of *deva*, *ṛṣi*, *manuṣya*, and so on. The *acetanas* include *prakṛti*, *kāla* (time), *karma*, *avidyā* (here as real veiling, not as Advaitic indeterminate), the *Vedas* themselves on Madhva's reading, and the *guṇa*-hierarchy.

The ordering is itself argument. To say of two real beings that one is *paratantra* on the other is to say something about which dependence-relations hold and in which direction. The *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna* compresses these dependence-relations into a list whose place-by-place structure is the school's settled ontology. Brevity and density coincide.

Madhva's claim in this text that "you are not Viṣṇu", drawing the contrast between the *jīva*-class and *svatantra-tattva*, should be taken at the grammatical level it has here. It is the denial that the dependent has the *svatantra-bhāva* of its locus of dependence, not a generic monism-denial of any kind whatsoever.

Jayatīrtha's *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna-Ṭīkā* unfolds the text into a sustained scholastic treatment.

engaged_works.title: Karma-Nirnaya
engaged_works.summary:
A short *prakaraṇa* on the doctrine of *karma*: its ontological status, its relation to *jñāna*, the conditions for *karmaphala* (the fruit of action), and the precise sense in which *karma* is *paratantra* on Viṣṇu's *anumati* (permission).

The argument moves on two fronts. Against the Mīmāṃsaka, who treats *karma* as a self-sufficient *apūrva*-generating mechanism (a posited unseen potency that secures the link between act and fruit), Madhva insists that all *karma-phala* arises only by Viṣṇu's *anumati*. *Karma* is real, but it is not autonomous; the link between act and fruit is not internal to the act. The *jīva* is genuinely *kartā* (agent), against the Sāṃkhya denial of agency to *puruṣa*, but only as instrument under the supreme *kartā*. Against the Advaitin, who treats *karma* as preparatory and ultimately *vyāvahārika* (transactionally real but not ultimately so), Madhva insists that *karma* is *pāramārthika*ally real and that the *karma-yoga* of the *Gītā* is constitutive of the soteriological path, not merely propaedeutic to it.

The text also fixes the school's analysis of *adṛṣṭa* (the unseen residue), of the distinction between *prārabdha* (begun-to-fruit) and *sañcita* (accumulated) *karma*, and of the *karma*-conditioned trajectory of *jīvas* under the *taratamya* of the *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna*.

Jayatīrtha's *Karma-Nirṇaya-Ṭīkā* is the standard sub-commentary.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Vivarana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Compact methodological gloss accompanying the Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; transmitted with the Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya in the Sarva-Mūla manuscript tradition.
engaged_works.summary:
A short methodological text laying out the *nyāya* (the *yukti-paddhati*, or method of reasoned construction) by which the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* proceeds. The *bhāṣya* states conclusions in compressed form; the *Anuvyākhyāna* mounts the dialectic; the *Nyāya-Vivaraṇa* articulates the meta-method.

Four topics are treated. First, how *adhikaraṇas* are parsed: the rules by which the five-fold *viṣaya–saṃśaya–pūrvapakṣa–siddhānta–saṅgati* structure is identified in a sequence of *sūtras*. Second, what counts as a *viṣaya-vākya* — the topic-statement that determines what an *adhikaraṇa* is about. Third, how *sūtras* function as *upakrama* (initial-frame) and *upasaṃhāra* (closing-frame) signals that fix the *tātparya* of intervening verses. Fourth, the conditions under which *śruti*, *yukti*, and *anubhava* converge as joint *pramāṇa*s, and what to do when they do not.

Madhva gives particular attention to the difference-*sūtras* of *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3, on which his realist reading of the text most depends. The rule that *upakrama* and *upasaṃhāra* fix the purport of intervening passages is what underwrites his rejection of Śaṅkara's reading of 2.3.43 as identity-with-modification: the framing *sūtras* of the *adhikaraṇa* are dependence-statements, and the intervening verses must be read in their light.

Jayatīrtha's gloss makes the text operational for later scholastic use.

engaged_works.title: Bhagavata-Tatparya-Nirnaya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored doctrinal exposition of the Bhāgavata.
engaged_works.summary:
A *tātparya-nirṇaya* on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa*: not a verse-by-verse commentary but a determination of doctrinal purport, made by selecting and glossing key verses to show that the *Bhāgavata* teaches *Tattvavāda* rather than any monist reading. The genre is what the *Gītā-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* and the *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* are.

Madhva treats the *Bhāgavata* as a *prasthāna*-text co-equal with *Upaniṣad*, *Brahma-Sūtra*, and *Gītā*. The elevation is unusual within Vedānta and anticipates by roughly three centuries the Gauḍīya use of the same text. Its consequence is that *Bhāgavata*-internal locutions become decisive for the school's theology.

The interpretive labour is concentrated on the *Bhāgavata*'s apparent identity-statements. *Ahaṁ sarvasya prabhavaḥ* (*Bhāg.* 10.85) and *vāsudevaḥ sarvam iti* (*Bhāg.* 11.5.49) are read as cosmological-dependence claims rather than as metaphysical-identity claims. The *līlās* of Kṛṣṇa are read as ontologically real and continuous with Viṣṇu's *svarūpa*, not as appearances on a deeper undifferentiated ground. *Bhakti* is fixed as the unique direct cause of *aparokṣa-jñāna* — the verse-anchors here become standard internal references for the school's later soteriology.

The text supplies many of the verse-citations on which subsequent Mādhva and Gauḍīya devotional metaphysics are built.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
dravyaṃ karma ca kālaśca svabhāvo jīva eva ca /
yadanugrahataḥ santi na santi yadupekṣayā //
narte tvat kriyate kiñcidityāderna hariṃ vinā /
jñānasvabhāvato 'pi syānmuktiḥ kasyāpi hi kvacit //
ajñānāṃ jñānado viṣṇurjñānināṃ mokṣadaśca saḥ /
ānandadaśca muktānāṃ sa evaiko janārdanaḥ //

pada_analysis.gloss: from the very nature of knowledge

pada_analysis.morphology: sandhi form (gen sg masc/neut pron + particle)

samasa_vigrahas.note: Ablative expressing causal basis: 'from the very nature of knowledge'.
Agent noun: one who gives knowledge.
Agent noun: one who gives liberation.
Agent noun: one who gives bliss.
Traditional epithet of Viṣṇu; the etymology is inherited rather than argumentative here.

karaka_structure.note: These coordinated nominatives are the subjects of santi.
Ablative of causal dependence: they exist through his favor.
Instrumental of causal non-support: they fail to exist through his disregard.
Object of kriyate in the impersonal passive statement.

key_passages.english_close: Substance, action, time, intrinsic nature, and the individual self too — these exist by whose favor. They do not exist by whose disregard. From statements such as 'nothing whatsoever is done without you', [the conclusion follows] that without Hari, liberation would not occur for anyone, anywhere — not even from the very nature of knowledge. Viṣṇu is the giver of knowledge to the ignorant. He is the giver of liberation to the knowers. He, the same one Janārdana, is also the giver of bliss to the liberated.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a fair programmatic statement of Madhva's mature position because it compresses his asymmetrical dependence-ontology and his soteriology into one argument: all categories depend on Viṣṇu, and even liberating knowledge is not self-sufficient apart from his grace. In the accessible corpus for this run, the Anuvyākhyāna is the clearest directly transcribable witness to that dependence claim.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext transformation of sa_mAdhva-anuvyAkhyAna.xml (version 2020-07-31), local file /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/madhva_anuvyakhyana_gretil.txt

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
jīveśvarabhidā caiva jaḍeśvarabhidā tathā /
jīvabhedo mithaścaiva jaḍajīvabhidā tathā /
mithaśca jaḍabhedo yaḥ prapañco bhedapañcakaḥ //
so 'yaṃ satyo hyanādiśca sādiścennāśamāpnuyāt /
dvaitaṃ na vidyata iti tasmādajñānināṃ matam //

pada_analysis.gloss: difference between the insentient and Īśvara
difference between insentient matter and jīva

samasa_vigrahas.note: The world-manifold is characterized as a set of five real differences.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of āpnuyāt in the conditional clause.
Object reached if the world of difference were beginningful.

key_passages.english_close: The difference between jīva and Īśvara, and likewise the difference between the insentient and Īśvara; the mutual difference among jīvas, and likewise the difference between insentient matter and jīva; and the mutual difference among insentient things: that world-manifold is the fivefold difference. This is real and beginningless indeed; if it had a beginning, it would come to destruction. Therefore the statement 'duality does not exist' is the view of the ignorant.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not merely a famous slogan-locus: it is Madhva's own compressed definition of what the world consists in, followed immediately by the claim that this differentiated manifold is real and beginningless. For a thinker whose system is organized around permanent difference, this is exactly the sort of establishing passage that deserves selection.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext transformation of sa_mAdhva-anuvyAkhyAna.xml (version 2020-07-31), local file /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/madhva_anuvyakhyana_gretil.txt

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
śuddhaḥ sākṣī yadā siddho duḥkhitvaṃ vāryate katham /
upajīvyapramāṇaṃ tad bhedagrāhakameva hi /
ato jīveśayorbhedaḥ śrutisāmarthyasusthiraḥ //
tathāpi tu cidānandapūrvāstatsadṛśā guṇāḥ /
sārasvarūpamasyāpi muktāvapyavaśiṣyate //
ato 'bhedavadevaitāḥ śrutayaḥ pravadanti hi /
paurāṇāni ca vākyāni sādṛśyābhedasaṃśrayāt //
sādṛśyācca pradhānatvāt svātantryādapicābhidām /
āhurīśena jīvasya na svarūpābhidāṃ kvacit //

pada_analysis.gloss: made very firm by the force of scripture
because they rest on a non-difference of similarity

samasa_vigrahas.note: A pramāṇa on which other interpretive claims must depend.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of vāryate in the rhetorical question.
That which is to be excluded once the pure witness is established.
Locative indicating the state in which the similarity remains.
Coordinated nominative subject with the śrutis.
What is asserted or denied by āhuḥ.

key_passages.english_close: When the pure witness-consciousness is established, how can its suffering-status be denied? That [witness-cognition] is indeed a foundational means of knowledge, one that apprehends difference. Therefore the difference between jīva and Lord is made very firm by the force of scripture. Yet, even so, qualities similar to that [Lord]'s — beginning with consciousness and bliss — remain. Even in liberation, they remain for this [jīva] as its essential form. Therefore these scriptural statements indeed speak as if of non-difference. The Purāṇic statements as well [speak so], because they rest on a non-difference grounded in similarity. And because of similarity, predominance, and also sovereignty, they apply a Lord-designation to the jīva. But they never apply a designation of essential identity.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This passage fairly represents Madhva because it shows how his ontology and hermeneutics lock together: difference is secured by the very pramāṇas that interpretation must presuppose, while identity-language is reassigned to similarity rather than essence. It also avoids caricature by acknowledging the real likeness of the liberated jīva to the Lord without collapsing that likeness into identity.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext transformation of sa_mAdhva-anuvyAkhyAna.xml (version 2020-07-31), local file /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/madhva_anuvyakhyana_gretil.txt

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
vairāgyato bhaktidārḍhyaṃ tenopāsā yadā bhavet /
āparokṣyaṃ bhaved viṣṇoriti pādakramo bhavet //

pada_analysis.morphology: sandhi form (inst sg neut pron + nom sg fem)
3sg opt ind P (surface sandhi form of bhavet)

samasa_vigrahas.note: Immediate realization, not mediate or remote cognition.

karaka_structure.note: Source from which firmness of bhakti arises.
Instrumental link: by that firmness, upāsanā occurs.
Genitive specifying whose immediate realization is attained.

verb_modality.note: All three uses carry sequential potential/gnomic force.

key_passages.english_close: From dispassion comes firmness of devotion; when thereby contemplative worship comes to be, immediate realization of Viṣṇu comes to be. Such is the sequence of the section.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This compact verse is worth selecting because it lays out the internal order of Madhva's path rather than merely praising devotion in general: vairāgya stabilizes bhakti, bhakti issues in upāsanā, and upāsanā culminates in immediate realization of Viṣṇu. That sequence is central to the school's account of how liberation is actually reached.
